coredata.dc:title: Bright red fluorescent amphiphilic carbon dots as dualphase and visual sensor for selective detection of As3+ in aqueous environment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Rajendran, Sathish
Bhunia, Susanta Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Increasing attention has been focused in the area of multi-mode sensing method for on-site application to avoid the false positive results and enhance the accuracy label measurements. This article reports the synthesis of bright red fluorescent amphiphilic carbon dots (C-dots) as dualphase fluorescent nanosensor for selective detection of As3+ in water. One-pot solvothermal approach was chosen for the C-dots synthesis with a maximum fluorescence quantum yield of 47%. Bright red fluorescence of the C-dots was selectively weakened upon the treatment with As3+, although the other competing ions showed infectivity. Static quenching was proved behind this fluorescence attenuation by absorbance and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. The amphiphilicity of synthesized C-dots has been utilized to detect As3+ in both liquid and solid phase. Aqueous dispersion of C-dots showed a dramatic fluorescence quenching efficiency of maximum 80%. To fabricate solid phase sensor, C-dots were successfully coated and absorbed on silica beads and cotton buds. These fluorescent solid matrices also displayed a relative good quenching efficiency (70–80%) towards As3+ without any leaching out of the C-dots from the solid surface. In addition, these C-dots showed an adequate potentiality to detect this ion in real water sample analysis. These findings would enable a great potentiality in the environmental water contaminants sensing technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red fluorescent amphiphilic C-dots
Selective detection of As3+
Liquid and solid phase senors
Hydrocoating
Absorption

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photostability and ionic strength measurements of AR-C-dots
Dual phase selective detection of As3+
Optical stability and Ionic interference studies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Sathish R. acknowledges VIT University for providing fellowship and seed money as a research grant. S. K. Bhunia expresses appreciation to “VIT SEED GRANT”, DST INSPIRE funding (DST/INSPIRE Faculty Award/2018/DST/ INSPIRE/04/2018/002484 ), and SERB ( SRG/2020/000645 ) for financial support to carry out this research work. The authors are grateful to Varsha U.V. for her assistance in data analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectrum of RA-C-dots. (B-D) XPS deconvoluted spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s, and P 2p showing different functional moieties on the C-dots surface.

(A) TEM image and (B) average particle size of RA-C-dots. (C) UV–vis absorption (violet color), fluorescent excitation (blue color), and emission spectra (red color) of AR-C-dots. (D) Emission spectra of AR-C-dots at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 350 to 600 nm.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of AR-C-dots by varying concentration of As3+ from 6 µM to 1.71 mM. (B) Fluorescence digital images of AR-C-dots with mentioned concentrations of As3+.
Schematic representation of hydrocoating process of RA-C-dots over hydrophobic silica surface.

Contact angle of (A) un-coated and (B) coated solid matrix with RA-C-dots. Emission spectra of RA-C-dots coated (C) silica beads and (D) cotton buds after the treatment of mentioned As3+ concentrations. Fluorescence digital images of RA-C-dots coated (E) silica beads and (F) cotton buds after the treatment of mentioned As3+ concentrations. (G) Daylight and fluorescence images of VIT written by RA-C-dots.

(A) FT-IR spectra of AR-C-dots and their interaction with As3+. (B) Life-time decay kinetic graph of AR-C-dots and AR-C-dots with As3+. (C) Particle size distribution of AR-C-dots before and after addition of As3+ at different concentrations. (D) Schematic diagram of plausible sensing mechanism.
A schematic illustration for the synthesis of red fluorescent amphiphilic C-dots.
Schematic representation of As3+ ion detection in liquid and solid phase using RA-C-dots.
A comparison study of literature survey for synthesized red fluorescent C-dots.

row.entry: Citric acid, p-phenylenediamine, and phytic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: On-site detection of As3+ in different water samples using RA-C-dots.

head.ce:title: Bright red fluorescent amphiphilic carbon dots as dualphase and visual sensor for selective detection of As3+ in aqueous environment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, 632014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Increasing attention has been focused in the area of multi-mode sensing method for on-site application to avoid the false positive results and enhance the accuracy label measurements. This article reports the synthesis of bright red fluorescent amphiphilic carbon dots (C-dots) as dualphase fluorescent nanosensor for selective detection of As3+ in water. One-pot solvothermal approach was chosen for the C-dots synthesis with a maximum fluorescence quantum yield of 47%. Bright red fluorescence of the C-dots was selectively weakened upon the treatment with As3+, although the other competing ions showed infectivity. Static quenching was proved behind this fluorescence attenuation by absorbance and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. The amphiphilicity of synthesized C-dots has been utilized to detect As3+ in both liquid and solid phase. Aqueous dispersion of C-dots showed a dramatic fluorescence quenching efficiency of maximum 80%. To fabricate solid phase sensor, C-dots were successfully coated and absorbed on silica beads and cotton buds. These fluorescent solid matrices also displayed a relative good quenching efficiency (70–80%) towards As3+ without any leaching out of the C-dots from the solid surface. In addition, these C-dots showed an adequate potentiality to detect this ion in real water sample analysis. These findings would enable a great potentiality in the environmental water contaminants sensing technology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Consumption of metal and non-metals is increasing day by day due to several man-kind activities and rapid industrialization. The poorly degradable and sustainable hazardous ions enter into the food chain and lead to bioaccumulation. The accumulated and non-accumulated ions are consumed directly by humans and other living organisms resulting in devastating effects due to the ionic toxicity with low metabolic activity [1]. Arsenic is one of the heavy metal ions and also known to be a poisonous inorganic substance with common oxidation state of + 3 and + 5 (arsenite & arsenate). The trivalent state of arsenic is the most hazardous form of ecologically accessible arsenic oxidation states [2]. For living beings, As3+ is 50 times more hazardous than As5+ because of its interactions with enzymes related to human metabolism [3]. Uncontrollable usage and mishandling of such hazardous contaminants make it an inevitable part of the aquatic system. According to WHO reports, the permissible limit of arsenic in drinking water is about 10 ppb [4]. Overwhelming of these pollutants can easily enter into human metabolic cycle through aspiration, consumption and skin contact that causes severe chronic and acute diseases [5]. Chronic diseases such as cell cancer, skin cancer, liver cancer, cardiovascular problems, hypotension, and organ failure can be caused even with a trace amount of arsenic ions exposure [6–8]. High levels of arsenic in the body can cause acute diseases such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, seizures, cancer, and nervous system damage [8]. As a result, it's more important to detect this type of pollutant in drinking water. There are couple of techniques including ion chromatography [9], atomic fluorescence spectrometry [10], coupled plasma mass spectrometry [11] and high-performance liquid chromatography [12] for the detection of As3+. Nevertheless, in terms of on-field applications, these approaches have several disadvantages such as typically necessitating huge equipment, high maintenance expenses, complicated operations and less portability as well as longer time-consuming [13]. In recent times many rapid detection methods have been developed including colorimetric [14], fluorimetric [15], electrochemical [16] and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [17] sensors. However, fluorescence-based sensors possess great consideration because of their cost-effectiveness, rapidness, nacked-eye detection and ease to handle which has led to their widespread adoption [18]. Numerous studies have been reported using fluorescent dyes and organic fluorophores, but these are lagged behind due to some major drawbacks such as high photo-bleaching, long time reaction, toxicity, high cost, and instability [19]. To overcome all these issues, fluorescent-based carbon dots (C-dots) were brought forward to detect the harmful As3+ due to their spectacular properties such as non-toxicity, eco-friendly, cost-effective, smaller in size (<10 nm), less-photobleaching, tunable fluorescent, easy surface modification and high photostability [20–22]. C-dots with graphitic sp2 carbon cores can be formed using a variety of synthetic techniques with different functional groups enabling them to dissolve in diverse solvent. A few works have been reported on the detection of As3+ using fluorescent C-dots in the aqueous medium to evolve water-based applications. The tediousness in portability and instability (long period) of aqueous-based C-dots limits their involvement in the on-field applications. The previous reports include over various phase fluorescent sensors including liquid [2], paper strips [23], silica aerogel [24], cellulose nanofibers [25] and intracellular nanosensors [26], were used to detect As3+. Generally, adsorption and incorporation of fluorescent nanoparticles in solid matrix usually found to have leaching problems in aqueous medium due to greater solubility in water, size mismatch between the fluorophore diameter and cavity of the porous nanostructures, improper adsorption on solid surfaces, etc. Therefore, development of a fluorescent novel sensor is highly appreciable that can overcome all of the mentioned barriers.

In this work, we report the synthesis of red fluorescent amphiphilic carbon dots (RA-C-dots) for selective and sensitive detection of As3+ in water in both liquid and solid phases. RA-C-dots were prepared via one-pot solvothermal synthesis from (formylmethyl)triphenylphosphonium chloride (TPP) as carbon precursor and acetone as solvent. The maximum fluorescence quantum yield was reported as 47%. The strong fluorescence properties have been described based on the formation of carbon core with retained unique chemical properties of TPP and its surface defects or defect sites [27]. The lipophilic nature of surface anchored TPP moieties imparts hydrophobicity to the C-dots. In addition, exterior surface of the C-dots reveals hydrophilic functional groups during synthesis. Thus, the resulting C-dots displayed amphiphilic in nature. This superior property has been utilized to detect As3+ in both liquid and solid phases. The C-dots aqueous solution underwent fluorescence quenching with the treatment of As3+ in the concentration ranging between 6 µM and 1.71 mM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2 µM. In case of solid sensor fabrication, hydrocoating and absorption techniques were applied to coat and captivate the RA-C-dots on silica beads and cotton fibers respectively. The resulting matrices displayed a magnificent fluorescence color transition upon the incubation of As3+ concentration ranging 0.49–1.71 mM without any leaching of the fluorophore. Formation of chelate complex between negatively charged oxygen containing functional groups on RA-C-dots surface and As3+ and electron transfer from C-dots to vacant 4p orbital of As3+ were predicted for C-dots fluorescence modulation. The quenching of RA-C-dots fluorescence was imputed to the static quenching that was proved by absorbance and fluorescence lifetime measurements. The liquid and solid phase sensors were used for on-site field As3+ detection by using different natural water samples including sea, river, industrial, and tap water. The quantitative spiking assay showed all water samples exhibited good recovery rates of As3+. These C-dots based dual-phase sensors will avoid the erroneous results in practical applications and also will open a new way to detect colorless As3+ in aqueous environment.

(Formylmethyl)triphenylphosphonium chloride, arsenic (III) iodide and deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO-d6) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. Ferrous sulfate heptahydrate, manganese (II) chloride tetrahydrate, acetone, ethanol, silica gel, sodium hydroxide, cadmium (II) chloride and β-cyclodextrin were acquired from SRL Pvt. Ltd. Calcium chloride anhydrous, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and lead (II) nitrate were purchased MPM Scientific Co. Cobalt chloride hexahydrate and nickel acetate tetrahydrate were acquired from LOBA Chemicals Pvt. Ltd. Mercury (II) acetate was purchased from Avra synthesis Pvt. Ltd. Dimethylformamide (DMF) was bought from Spectrochem chemicals Pvt. Ltd. Polydimethoxysiloxane (PDMS) was acquired from Dow-corning Sylgard. All the chemicals were used for synthesis and analysis without further purification.

UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy was measured by JASCO UV–visible spectrophotometer (V-730). C-dots excitation and emission spectra were recorded using Synergy H1 - H1M multimode fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed using SHIMADZU spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured via ESCA+ Omicron Nanotechnology Oxford Instruments. The synthesized C-dots were drop-casted on graphene-coated copper grid and 200 kV JEOL JEM-2100 F microscope were used to capture transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. A Horiba Yobin (DeltaFlex-01) spectrophotometer was used to determine the fluorescent decay and time-dependent single photon counting. D2 Phaser desktop/max-RAX-ray diffractometer was used to measure C-dots crystallinity. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra was measured using 400 MHz (Bruker) NMR Spectrometer. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was measured by Horiba Scientific SZ 100 instrument.

RA-C-dots were prepared by one-step solvothermal synthesis. Briefly, 100 mg of TPP was dissolved in 3 mL of acetone and the mixture was sonicated for 5 min to get unique dispersion. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 5 h. It resulted to reddish-brown solution and the product was purified by column chromatography using ethanol as solvent. Next, the ethanolic solution was evaporated via rotary evaporator and the obtained C-dots were used for further characterizations and analysis.

Silica beads were fabricated from PDMS polymer. Briefly, 5 g of PDMS and 0.5 mL of curing agent were mixed in a 20 mL glass vial. Then, the mixture was stirred constantly for 10 min followed by keeping under a vacuum atmosphere to remove the oxygen bubbles. Finally, the obtained clear polymer slurry was kept for drying at 70 °C for 3 h. The acquired silica polymer beads were cut into 1 cm x 1 cm in length and width to make a rough solid surface.

Fluorescent silica beads and cotton buds were prepared by two approaches including hydrocoating and absorption process respectively. Typically, 250 mg of sodium hydroxide and 500 mg of cyclodextrin (CD) were dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water taken in a 100 mL beaker and the mixture was stirred for 10 min to get an uniform solution. Further, the prepared silica beads were immersed into the solution containing RA-C-dots. This mixture was stirred for 15 min for uniform coating of RA-C-dots on the surface of silica beads. In case of fluorescent cotton buds synthesis, the cotton buds were initially soaked into the chloroform dispersed C-dots (abbreviated as “CR-C-dots”) solution to get the complete coating of preferred C-dots via hydrophobicity assisted core absorption due to the hydrophobic properties of CR-C-dots. Moreover, the fluorescence leaching was completely negotiated during the attachment of CR-C-dots into cotton buds via absorption technique and the florescence stability was withstanded all along the sensing experiments. Finally, these fluorescent silica beads and cotton buds were used to sense the toxic As3+ in water.

RA-C-dots were prepared by a one-pot solvothermal method using TPP as carbon precursor and acetone as solvent. During the reaction, the yellow color solution turned into reddish-brown indicating the genesis of C-dots. The synthesized RA-C-dots solution was purified with the help of silica gel (60–120 mesh) and ethanol as eluent. The obtained solution mixture was evaporated to dryness using a rotary evaporator. After complete removal of solvent, the obtained crude product showed bright red fluorescence under irradiation of hand-held UV lamp. The strong lipophilic nature of the TPP molecule encourages to solubilize the C-dots in various solvents [27]. The amphiphilic nature of these C-dots was examined by dissolving the C-dots in different solvents, such as aqueous and non-aqueous (chloroform) solvents. Aqueous dispersed C-dots (abbreviated as “AR-C-dots”) were excited in different wavelengths and showed the maximum emission wavelength at 595 nm under the best excitation wavelength of 500 nm. Whereas, chloroform dispersed C-dots (CR-C-dots) displayed a strong emission peak at 620 nm under the excitation wavelength of 540 nm. The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was measured by using Rhodamine B as a standard dye and the maximum yield was calculated as 20% and 47% of C-dots dispersed in water and chloroform respectively. Typically, these C-dots showed extremely high dispersibility in chloroform because of surface associated hydrophobic TPP moieties that led to very bright fluorescence and quantum yield [28]. Schematic representation of synthetic strategies and digital images of RA-C-dots have been shown in Scheme 1.

The formation of such bright red fluorescent C-dots depends on several factors. Presence of three benzene rings in selected TPP molecule helped easy carbonization during heating and contributed in the extension of conjugation within the C-dots matrix lead to red shifting fluorescence nature. The presence of oxygen from TPP and acetone also plays a vital role for contributing fluorescence to the C-dots during the carbonization process [29]. Longer growth time and high temperature heating also contributed to the appearance of red fluorescence. The bright fluorescence property was considered as a combined effect of the variation of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon ratio, generation of defect sites from the hetero atoms such as oxygen and phosphorous. The importance of acetone for the formation of red fluorescent C-dots was examined in the synthesis strategy. Other solvents such as H2O, DMSO, DMF, and EtOH were used to synthesize C-dots in place of acetone. A maximum of green and yellow fluorescence was observed from the prepared C-dots with low brightness as shown in Fig. S1. This indicates a significant role of acetone in the carbonization process. There are some reports where red fluorescent C-dots were synthesized from varied precursors and processes ( Table 1) [30–32], [20], [33]. However, our presented C-dots showed comparable and significant with respect to fluorescence brightness and reaction parameters.

The amphiphilic nature mainly depends on the chemical composition and surface functionalities of RA-C-dots. FT-IR and XPS spectra were used to determine the surface functional groups and elements of RA-C-dots. These RA-C-dots exhibit oxygen and phosphorous containing functional groups in the FT-IR spectrum ranging from 4000 to 400 cm−1 as shown in Fig. 1A. The following characteristic peaks were observed in RA-C-dots. Stretching vibrations of O-H and C-H were positioned at 3340 cm−1 and 3053 cm−1 respectively. Symmetric stretching vibration of CO and bending vibration of O-H from carboxylic groups appeared at 1618 cm−1 and 999 cm−1 respectively [34,35]. The peaks centered at 1529 cm−1 and 689 cm−1 correspond for respective stretching and bending vibrations of CC. The stretching vibrations of C-O/C-OH appeared at 1105 cm−1 [36]. The presence of phosphorus moieties including P-C, P = O and PO4 3- were confirmed by the respective existence of peaks at 1440, 1317 and 531 cm−1 [27,37]. The asymmetric CO-O-C stretching of phospholipids was observed at 1170 cm−1 [38]. XPS analysis indicates the existence of different elements such as C, O, P and Cl on the surface of RA-C-dots as shown in survey spectrum (Fig. S2). The C 1 s deconvoluted spectra in Fig. 1B revealed two peaks at 284.0 and 288.1 eV for respective sp2 hybridized CC/C-C and CO [21,24]. The O 1 s deconvoluted spectra showed two peaks at 531.4 and 532.9 eV correspond to –COOH/C-O-C and C-OH (Fig. 1C) [39,40]. The two peaks at 131.5 and 132.5 eV denote the existence of P-C and P-O bonds in the deconvoluted spectrum of P 2p as shown in Fig. 1D [41,42]. To determine the crystallinity, the synthesized RA-C-dots were examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy and the broad amorphous peak exist at 24.2˚ for respective [002] lattice plane shown in Fig. S3 [43].

The morphology of fluorescent RA-C-dots was tested using TEM study. The TEM image in Fig. 2A clearly showed the formation of spherical-shaped nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm. The average size diameter of the C-dots was calculated from the image using image J software and showed the value of 3.1 ± 1.2 nm (Fig. 2B). The AR-C-dots exhibited five absorbance peaks positioned at 268, 278, 325, 384 and 506 nm in UV–visible spectra. The two peaks at 268 and 278 nm corresponded for parental molecular residues that attach on C-dots surface [44]. The attachment of precursor moieties on the C-dots surface occurs during carbonization process of the synthesis and is known as structural memory. The absorbance peaks at 325 and 384 nm were ascribed to π -π * transition of aromatic sp2 moieties [45]. The peak at 506 nm indicates n- π * transitions of CO and C-O groups present in the surface state [46].

AR-C-dots exhibited a strong emission peak at 595 nm under the excitation of 500 nm wavelength in fluorescence spectral analysis shown in Fig. 2C. Further, the AR-C-dots were excited at a variety of wavelengths ranging from 350 to 600 nm and their respective emission spectra were shown in Fig. 2D. The emission peak remained the same under all the excitations wavelengths. This phenomenon indicates the relative uniformity and narrow distribution of surface energy states [47]. Similarly, the RA-C-dots were dissolved in chloroform and excited in different wavelengths between 350 and 600 nm. Fig. S4 displayed a maximum emission peak at 620 nm under 540 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability and ionic strength measurements of AR-C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: For the photostability experiments, AR-C-dots solution was irradiated under UV light of 365 nm excitation wavelength for 90 min. During the light irradiation process, aliquots were taken every 30 min and emission spectra were measured. No changes were observed upon exposure of continuous 1 h as shown in Fig. S5 from both fluorescence spectra and digital images. Only a very slight attenuation of fluorescence intensity was observed after 90 min light exposure. But the change was quite insignificant. This experiment demonstrated excellent photostability of AR-C-dots. To measure ionic strength stability, different concentrations of NaCl aqueous solution ranging between 0.05 M and 0.32 M was incubated with AR-C-dots. A non-significant fluorescence changes were observed with increase in NaCl concentration as shown in Fig. S6. It indicates their excellent ionic strength stability and salt tolerance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual phase selective detection of As3+
ce:section.ce:para: The target of these synthesized RA-C-dots was selective recognition of As3+ in water. To implement this concept, the C-dots were used as liquid and solid-based sensors. Both the sensors displayed a remarkable fluorescence changes after the treatment with As3+. Scheme 2 highlights the easy and effective perception in both solution and solid phases.

The purpose for RA-C-dots synthesis was to investigate the selective and sensitive recognition towards toxic As3+. The C-dots aqueous dispersion (AR-C-dots) were incubated with different metal and non-metal ions such as As3+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Hg2+ , and Cd2+ at a concentration of 1.71 mM and the fluorescence spectra were measured immediately. Surprisingly, it was noticed that the fluorescence intensity of C-dots reduced to ∼80% upon the treatment with As3+. Whereas, no significant changes were perceived in case of the other ions. Notably, around 40% decrease of C-dots fluorescence intensity was observed in case of Hg2+ treatment. However, Fig. S7A clearly shows a noteworthy depletion of C-dots’ fluorescence intensity for As3+. UV–vis absorbance spectrum of C-dots were also measured after adding the ions. A clear visible change was recorded especially for As3+ and the other ions completely failed to commute the absorbance properties of C-dots as shown in Fig. S7B.

Various concentrations of As3+ were treated with AR-C-dots to determine limit of detection (LOD) and quenching constant (Ksv). Fig. 3A showed a gradual fluorescence decrement with increasing the concentration of As3+ ranging from 6 µM to 1.71 mM and the extent of quenching was calculated as ∼80%. Fluorescence digital images of RA-C-dots solution were captured before and after the addition of As3+ different concentrations. It is very clear that the C-dots emission brightness also gradually decreased with increase in the analyte concentration as shown in Fig. 3B and it corroborates with the variation of emission spectra. The linear graph plotted using quencher concentration vs fluorescence intensity has been shown in Fig. S8 and it showed a good linearity with co-correlation value of R2 = 0.9969 and LOD of 2 µM. The results are comparable with the previous reports of As3+ detection. A comparative study has been presented in Table S1. Some of the cases, LOD values were reported lower . But, the detection was carried out in liquid phase. And, some of the reports consist of expensive instrument, time-consuming process, usage of toxic materials and dyes that limit their frequent usage and fast response. The quenching constant (KSV) was calculated as 8.5 × 104 M−1 using the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. 1).
(1) I0/I =1+Ksv [C]
where I0 and I correspond the fluorescence emission intensity of AR-C-dots before and after the addition of the quencher. [C] denotes the quencher concentration.
The value of KSV provides the detail about the binding affinity between AR-C-dots functional groups and quencher. The calculated KSV value indicates the sturdy binding of As3+ with AR-C-dots [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical stability and Ionic interference studies
ce:section.ce:para: AR-C-dots’ optical stability was examined by incubating at different temperature and pH towards the effect of As3+ detection. The C-dots’ aqueous solution was incubated at different temperatures ranging between 10 and 60 °C before and after the addition of As3+. The result in Fig. S9A reveals no significant changes in As3+ recognition at varied temperatures. Similarly, C-dots were incubated with different pH buffer solutions from 3 to 10 prior to and after the addition of As3+. Recognition capability of As3+ was very significant at all pH as shown in Fig. S9B. These results indicate the surface functional groups activeness at varied conditions that help to detect As3+. Interference studies of AR-C-dots were performed using different ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+ and Pb2+ to show the selectivity. Initially, all the above mentioned ions were mixed with AR-C-dots solution and the mixture was instantaneously executed fluorescence spectroscopic analysis to find out any changes of emission intensity. It was observed that no significant fluorescence quenching occurred with all the above ions. Afterwards, As3+ was added into the mixture containing both C-dots and noninterfering ions and the fluorescence was re-examined. Fig. S10 revealed an uninterrupted detection of As3+ in the solution mixture. These results confirmed that AR-C-dots showed excellent selectivity towards As3+ in presence of other competing ions.

This process has been utilized for uniform coating of RA-C-dots on hydrophobic solid silica surface for the fabrication of fluorescent solid sensor. Cyclodextrin (CD) has a hydrophobic inner cavity with hydrophilic functional groups decorated on the exterior surface. The outer functionalities of CD help to solubilize in aqueous medium. In order to imbibe the process of complete coating of C-dots over the solid surface, the idea of hydrocoating using CD has been applied. The outer hydrophilic functionality of CD converts to the aliphatic groups by the deprotonation in basic condition and made them to expose the inner hydrophobic aromatic moieties outer wards. This excellent feature will increase the repulsion among the aliphatic or aromatic groups of C-dots that comes in contact with the hydrophobic aromatic wall of CD. Furthermore, the addition of C-dots over CD in basic condition activates the repulsive interaction among the C-dot solution and CD itself to promote the stretchable non immersive C-dots film over its surface [49,50]. Briefly, the deprotonation of the functional groups present at the external wall of CD brought the quality of inner hydrophobic character to unveil. Further, the glucose moieties present at the wall of CD act to provide a “Buoyancy effect” from the hindrance force that arose in between bulk aromatic moieties of TPP and aliphatic chains of CD [51]. The combined action of buoyancy force and force of attraction made use to coat uniformly the RA-C-dots over the silica matrix, this coating method is called as “Hydrocoating”. This process has been schematically illustrated in Fig. 4.

In order to prove the hydrophobic-hydrophobic repulsive interaction plays a major role in the hydrocoating process, we have made three types of C-dots such as hydrophilic [27], hydrophobic, and amphiphilic prepared from TPP. Those were experimented and their respective details have been given in Fig. S11A-C. The hydrophilic TPP derived C-dots were found to completely dissolve in the mixture of alkaline CD solution. This was attributed to the hydrogen bonding between oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the C-dots surface with hydroxyl groups of CD molecules. Hydrophobic C-dots synthesized from TPP showed completely immiscible in the basic CD aqueous solution due to the hydrophobic-hydrophilic repulsive forces. But, this force lacks to provide enough potency to coat the hydrophobic C-dots over a solid matrix with sustainable fluorescence without leaching out. This limitation has been bridled by put forwarding two opposite driving forces together by incorporating amphiphilic RA-C-dots into the alkaline CD solution. The hydrophilic groups of amphiphilic C-dots assist downward movement of the C-dots into the aqueous phase via hydrogen bonding. Simultaneously, the hydrophobic end of the RA-C-dots pulls up upward by the repulsive interactions. These simultaneous upward and downward forces enabled the coating of amphiphilic C-dots perfectly over a solid matrix as shown in Fig. S11D. This proves that either downward or upward force alone can’t make the coating process successful.

The applicability of solid matrix sensors is more practicable and reliable compared to liquid sensors. For real applications, solid sensors are more useful than a liquid due to stability and portability. Here two kinds of solid matrix such as PDMS silica beads and cotton fiber have been chosen for As3+ recognition. A solid matrix made from PDMS and molded to form a rough surface by cutting them into 1 cm x 1 cm in length and width. The rough surface of these silica beads allow easy coating of RA-C-dots and high sensitivity. To determine the wettability, contact angle of RA-C-dots uncoated and coated silica matrix was measured. The uncoated and coated matrix showed a contact angle of water at 96.9˚ and 80.5˚ respectively as shown in Fig. 5A-B. It confirms that the hydrophobic nature of the silica matrix surface became reduced after RA-C-dots coating. It indicates that the functional groups of C-dots are present and exposed on the surface of the silica matrix. The RA-C-dots coated silica beads were immerged in aqueous solution of As3+ in different concentrations. The functional groups of the solid fluorescent matrix interact with As3+ via electron transfer process resulted in changes of fluorescence property. Emission spectra of the beads were measured under the best excitation wavelength of RA-C-dots and showed a gradual decrease of fluorescence intensity with increase in As3+ concentration (Fig. 5C). Fluorescence digital images in Fig. 5E endorsed the same scenario with the emission spectra.

Cotton fiber was taken as another solid matrix that has a general osmosis property as well as tendency to absorb water, oil, and other solvents efficiently. Based on this excellent property, the cotton bud was soaked in chloroform solution of RA-C-dots (CR-C-dots). Hydrophobic functionalities of the C-dots easily absorb on the cotton fiber without any leaching out during washing and accustomed for sensing experiments. Fig. 5D represents the attenuation of cotton bud’s fluorescence intensity with respect to As3+ concentrations. Fluorescence digital images were also captured and clearly showed a gradual decrease in emission brightness (Fig. 5F). Notably, approximate 70–80% fluorescence intensity got reduced for both the solid phase fluorophores after incubation with As3+. This is extremely significant especially in terms of leaching and photobleaching issues. The silica beads and cotton bud were immerged in water for continuous 12 h and images were captured. It showed that there is intact of fluorescence brightness without leaching in solution as evidenced in Fig. S12A-B. Apart from the detection of As3+, RA-C-dots were implemented in fluorescent ink application owing to their excellent photostability and high fluorescence QY. The dispersed CR-C-dots were used as ink and the words “VIT” were written on non-fluorescent paper. The words were illuminated under daylight and 365 nm wavelength UV lamp. Fig. 5G displays the clear visuality and bright red emission from the words.

The plausible sensing mechanism was explained via FT-IR spectra elucidation. The surface of RA-C-dots contains functional groups such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxyl. These negatively charged oxygen functional groups can chelate strongly with positively charged As3+ to form a co-ordination complex [34]. Apart from complex formation, AR-C-dots comprised of rich electron cloud aromatic moieties on their surface that can effectively donate the electron pairs to vacant 4p orbital of As3+. The 4p orbital of As3+ is completely vacant as it readily participates in the chelating process by accepting the electrons to fill up the orbital. It is evident from the FT-IR spectra as a huge reduction of all the electron donating groups during the inclusion of As3+ ( Fig. 6A). The exceptional broadening of hydroxyl and asymmetric carboxyl groups was observed after the addition of As3+ justifies the chelating complex formation [52].

This fluorescence modulation was ascribed to static quenching because of the ground state complex emergence between the quencher and C-dots. During complexation, the excited electrons got trapped by a quencher and it makes a non-fluorescent complex. The changes in absorbance spectra in Fig. S13 were noticed due to strong electronic transitions (π-π * and n-π * ). This phenomenon also impart the role of functional groups with the analyte. For further authentication of static quenching, the fluorescence lifetime of AR-C-dots was measured before and after the addition of As3+. The high concentration of As3+ (1.71 mM) was used to determine the lifetime decay. Surprisingly, unchanged nature of lifetime decay and kinetics was obtained as shown in Fig. 6B. The lifetime decay of AR-C-dots was calculated as 3.28 and 3.19 ns prior to and after addition of As3+ respectively. The unchanged nature of fluorescence lifetime and changed nature of UV–visible absorbance prove static quenching [53].

Furthermore, NMR spectral analysis was performed to endorse the participation of functional groups of RA-C-dots in As3+ sensing. The RA-C-dots were dispersed in DMSO-d6 and incubated with 1.71 mM of As3+. Next, 31P NMR spectroscopy was performed for both incubated and non-incubated C-dots solutions. The spectral results of 31P NMR confirm the active involvement of TPP phosphorous residues and functional groups of C-dots in co-ordination complex formation with As3+ via the shifting (downfield) of peak positioned at 25.5–27.6 ppm as shown in Fig. S14 [54]. Further, the hydrodynamic diameter of AR-C-dots was measured before and after incubation of As3+ with various concentrations. As shown in Fig. 6C, the particle diameter of AR-C-dots showed an average value of 10.9 nm. The value increased to 19.4 and 30.5 nm after the addition of 0.37 mM and 1.71 mM of As3+ respectively. This indicates the interaction of As3+ with C-dots functional groups resulting in aggregation of AR-C-dots. Fig. 6D demonstrates the plausible electrons transfer process of RA-C-dots to the vacant 4p orbital of As3+. The photo-induced electrons gathered in the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) instead of return back to the ground state of the C-dots. The residing electrons in the metastable state got transferred to the vacant 4p orbital of the As3+ by the captivation and forms the ground state complex resulting in the filling of the 4p orbital of the As3+. After the complex formation, the excited electrons are unable to return to the ground state (HOMO) and resulting in fluorescence quenching [55].

To investigate the practical applicability of RA-C-dots, various water samples including seawater, industry water, river water, and tap water were collected. All the water samples were filtered using cellulose syringe filter (0.22 µm) to get rid-off large sized particulates. Afterwards, RA-C-dots were dispersed in all water samples and used to sense As3+. The conventional quantitative spiking method was used to estimate the As3+ recovery rate in real water samples. Initially, a certain concentration of As3+ was added to RA-C-dots containing real water sample followed by instantaneous fluorescence measurements. All real water samples showed a good recovery rate ranging between 98.5% and 103% with good relative standard deviation as shown in Table 2. Furthermore, RA-C-dots coated fluorescent silica beads were used to assess the efficacy in real water As3+ detection. Here, also a good recovery rate ranging between 91.3% and 99% with good relative standard deviation was observed as shown in Table S2. It clearly shows that RA-C-dots provide high sensitivity and recovery rates towards As3+ despite the presence of other salts or ions in real samples.

Herein, we successfully synthesized bright red fluorescent amphiphilic C-dots by a one-step solvothermal process. These RA-C-dots showed excellent solubility in aqueous and non-aqueous solvents due to the presence of lipophilic and hydrophilic surface functional groups and were used to detect toxic As3+ in water via liquid and solid phases. The sensitivity and selectivity of AR-C-dots towards As3+ were estimated by varying the ion concentration in the liquid medium. The fluorescence intensity was gradually decreased with respect to the concentrations of As3+ and the quenching response was observed about ∼ 80% at a concentration of 1.71 mM with a significant LOD value. Other competing metals and non-metals were employed in the sensing experiment and showed silent nature. The quenching mechanism was attributed as static quenching with aid to life-time decay and UV–vis absorbance spectra. The functional group interactions and electron transference observed from FT-IR analysis further strengthen the quenching mechanism. In terms of portability and on-site application, RA-C-dots were employed in different solid matrix such as silica beads and cotton buds. The functional groups of RA-C-dots coated and absorbed over silica matrix and cotton buds could be implement direct contact with the hazardous ions and showed a remarkable recognition of As3+ in aqueous medium. This dual-phase fluorescent sensor provides a new avenue to detect As3+ in water.

Sathish Rajendran: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Susanta Kumar Bhunia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Sathish R. acknowledges VIT University for providing fellowship and seed money as a research grant. S. K. Bhunia expresses appreciation to “VIT SEED GRANT”, DST INSPIRE funding (DST/INSPIRE Faculty Award/2018/DST/INSPIRE/04/2018/002484), and SERB (SRG/2020/000645) for financial support to carry out this research work. The authors are grateful to Varsha U.V. for her assistance in data analysis.
